# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Good movies where crazy stuff just doesn't stop happening?

**Score:** 49
**Body:** I read somewhere that "Godzilla: Final Wars" is an insane movie that just doesn't stop, and I think that might be true considering the amount of stuff in the synopsis, and that got me really excited to watch it.

So I was wondering, what are some insane movies that just don't stop?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pulp Fiction is all about the crazy. There at least 50 crazy things that happen.
  - Yeah that movie is pretty fucking far from OK
2. It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World (1963)
3. Mad Max: Fury Road!!!
4. Bullet Train

Crank

Crank: High Voltage

Hardcore Henry
5. The Game (1997)
6. Beau Is Afraid

The Holy Mountain

Hardcore Henry

Hundreds of Beavers
  - i trust your other recomendations because you said hard core henry
7. Everything Everywhere All at Once (2022)
8. After Hours

## 2. Movies about writing?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Looking for recommendations for movies tangentially about writing, or where writing is a predominant component. For example, Capote, Dead Poets Society, Midnight in Paris… 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Adaptation (2002)

Kill Your Darlings (2013)

The End of the Tour (2015)
2. Barton Fink (1991)
3. Misery
4. Stranger Than Fiction. Orange County.
5. Finding Forester
6. Secret window
7. Naked Lunch
8. Throw Momma from the Train
9. Adaptation, Seven Psychopaths, Misery
10. Misery is a fantastic one.

## 3. Movies with creative/horrific deaths

**Score:** 9
**Body:** You know, people biting it in unique ways, not just beheadings or gunshots. 

Mostly in the vein of horror but am open to any genre as long as there’s some cool action!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Final Destination movies
  - Those set pieces are so well crafted. Like an immersive, horrific Rube Goldberg creation.
2. Lots of interesting deaths in Cabin in the Woods
3. Fargo
4. Quint's demise in Jaws.
5. The Indiana Jones movies have some creative deaths.
6. The Monkey it just came out this year . It had some really interesting deaths .
7. Has Bone Tomahawk been mentioned?

That or Hostel 1 & 2
8. - Total Recall (1990)
- The Road
- Oldboy (2003)
- Hereditary
9. Hot Fuzz

## 4. Movies like The Ritual

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I want to watch a movie like the ritual with an eerie atmosphere, taking place in/near a forest setting, with psychological and deeper scary themes.  

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Witch (2015)
  - One of my all time faves. A master class in slow burn suspense and mystery. Filmaker specifically chose an unusual aspect ratio to be sure the forest was represented properly.
2. Midsommar (2019, U.S./Sweden)  not strictly Nordic-made but filmed in Sweden and full of rituals, white-clad villagers, and daylight horror.
  - Incredibly atmospheric... Great film that slowly unravels  an interesting mystery. The sound adds so much to the intensity.
3. The Blair Witch Project.
4. The wailing
5. loop track (2023) is great
6. The Forest (2016)

Wrong Turn (2021)
7. Ravenous (1999)


Apocolyto (2006)
8. The Witch  
Blair Witch Project

## 5. Halloween movie suggestions? Teenage high school, maybe thriller?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** hi all! so sorry, im sure u get these 100 times a day. i really want to watch some spooky movjes, as its october, but i dont rlly have any ideas. the only halloween movie i watch every year is halloweentown (and sometimes hocus pocus). i like coming of age and like high schooly movies, so im thinking something along those lines. maybe more so thriller? idk! i’d prefer it to be in like the 80s, sometime around there. i loveeee psychological thrillers, but thats not 100% what im looking for here. while some ppl go to chat gpt, i come to. any recommendations? 

btw if something matches my description and its superrr well known, still suggest it! i’m in a movie slump and have barely watched any spooky films anyway.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. - Disturbing Behavior 
- The Faculty 
- The Craft
- It Follows 
- Jennifer’s Body
- Donnie Darko 
- Carrie
2. Fun Size (2012)
3. *Final Destination (2000)*
4. Carrie (1976) isn't set at Halloween, but it overlaps well with the things you're looking for.
5. The original Scream
6. 

The Stepfather (1987)

Christine (1983)

Silver Bullet (1985)

Once Bitten (1985)

The Lost Boys (1987)
7. Disturbing behavior
8. Ginger Snaps! It even has a Halloween party in it.
9. Ginger snaps
10. Foxfire

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which 2:30:00+ movie has you completely engaged the whole way through?

**Score:** 5488
**Body:** Amadeus(1984) 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I used to put on movies very low to help me sleep when I left the city because I was so used to street noise. One night I put on Amadeus then fell asleep. I woke up on the middle of the night to pitch black then suddenly Mozart’s laugh in the darkness and I almost had a fucking heart attack.
2. Saving Private Ryan, just to throw one in here that hasn't been said yet.
  - After getting through the 30 minute reenactment of the D-Day landing, which is a good reminder that war is hell, you have a remarkable movie about soldiers who were members of the Greatest Generation. Re-watch really hit different the ending, no spoilers
3. Casino.  I don't think I ever blinked.
4. Looking at these comments, I guess anything by Scorsese?
5. Amadeus
  - My local symphony showed the movie and played the score live. No way was I going to miss that. It was awesome!
6. Inglorious Basterds. Saw it in theater when it came out.
  - I remember seeing this when there were still midnight releases, and the entire theater was filled with Tarantino fans that laughed and cheered at all the right parts. It was one of the best theater experiences I've ever had.
7. Goodfellas

## 2. Name a character who isn’t the main villain of a movie, but is more unlikable than the actual villain

**Score:** 876
**Body:** Uncle Frank from Home Alone

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Al’s boss in Die Hard
  - Coked up Ellis

"Hans, bubbie..."
2. Percy from Green Mile
Dolores from Harry Potter to name a couple
  - Dolores Umbridge is a great one. One of the most unlikeable characters ever.
3. Walter Peck in Ghostbusters
  - He actually made good points though!
4. Denethor steward of Gondor

The corporate douchebag played by Giovanni Ribisi in Avatar.

Tom Cruise in Topic Thunder (but since the film is a critique on filmmaking, he arguably is the main villain).

Lt. Gorman in Aliens
5. Bogs. Shawshank Redemption
6. Jar Jar Binks
7. Grandpa. FUCKIN. Joe. From Willy Wonka.

## 3. RIP, Diane Keaton💔

**Score:** 620

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "I never understood the idea that you're supposed to mellow as you get older. Slowing down isn't something I relate to at all. The goal is to continue in good and bad, all of it."... R.I.P.
2. She made me laugh for over 50 years.  I guess it's ok to cry a bit at this news...
3. Loved her in Annie Hall and the Godfather movies ofc. RIP.
4. Rip she was a fantastic actress
5. Sad news
6. Ladida ladida 

RIP
7. 😱😭no!!
8. 💔💔💔
9. Damn it this is awful news!! I loved her 😔 RIP

## 4. Which film has the most gloomy conclusion?

**Score:** 434

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Se7en
2. Requiem for a Dream, many characters worth of gloom wrapped up in epic score that leaves you traumatized and hopeless.
3. Dancer in the Dark
4. The Boy in the Striped Pajamas
5. The Mist.
6. Das Boot.
7. Leaving Las Vegas
8. Manchester by the sea. Pitch black from beginning to end
9. Grave of the Fireflies. It is a continuous downward spiral with the only glimmer of hope being at the very beginning of the movie when the kids are ghosts at peace.
10. Boys Don't Cry

## 5. What upcoming movie are you NOT excited for?

**Score:** 406

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Proper version of the story? The old one is like the video game Smash TV which is really nothing like the King story.
  - What they did to Under the Dome making it into a TV show was terrible. It could have been an awesome miniseries or even two seasons but they turned it into cash grab bull shit and it got cancelled after season 3. 


When I started it I read it was a "complete series" but turns out it got cancelled before season 3 so they tied up loose ends and made it end. 


I never finished season 2. 
    - Simpsons did it better anyways.
    - "it was a mercy killing". 

Season one seemed fairly in line with the theme of the book, where the dome is the mystery and it's more about interpersonal conflicts, sort of like the mist. 

When season 3 rolled around I couldn't remember if I had even finished 2 so I watched the premiere. Oh actual alien pods. That's a choice. Would use a spoiler tag but if I spoiled it for someone, I did them a favor.
2. It’s Writer/Director, Edgar Wright…. If you know his body of work, how could you NOT be excited for this!?!
3. I’m cautiously optimistic. King adaptations have had a good track record so far this year, and if Wright has the balls to keep the original ending that will earn it a lot of points (in my eyes anyway).
  - Edgar Wright is a fantastic director, and that cast is spectacular.

Only the script can let it down.
4. Why does Reddit have such a hate boner for Glen Powell lmao
  - Here's the problem:


Redditors complain that there are no new male movie stars. No new Will Smith, or Tom Cruise, or Brad Pitt, or Leonardo DiCaprio, and the same guys from 20 years ago are still starring in all the movies.


"Okay," says Hollywood. "Here's Michael B. Jordan, and Glen Powell, and Austin Butler, and Timothee Chalamet. They've got some career momentum going, let's put them in stuff and see if they catch on with audiences."


Redditors notice that these specific actors are being cast in stuff a lot. They get butthurt at the idea that these new stars are being 'forced' on them because, you see, they think new stars should happen *organically*.


Sure, in the hundred year history of Hollywood, it's literally never happened that way and literally every big star they could possibly name became a box office draw because they had a lot of money and publicity put into making them a thing. But for some reason, now it's just supposed to happen naturally.


You know, like the whole world got together after Top Gun came out and cast a vote on whether Tom Cruise got to be the biggest movie star in the world now or not.


The movie subs all *loved* Glen Powell when Top Gun: Maverick came out. People were always talking about how he should have been a bigger star, and how good he was in Scream Queens and Everybody Wants Some. 


Then he had back to back hits with Anyone But You and Twisters, and now he's getting attached to various upcoming projects, so suddenly he's "being shoved down our throats" because the second an underrated actor has some success, they're "overexposed."
5. Avatar part xxxx

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What is it about The Shining that makes the movie itself feel so haunted?

**Score:** 120
**Body:** I’ve seen many scary movies in my life, but The Shining is different. Not only does the movie make me uneasy, but it feels like the movie itself is haunted. Sure, a movie such as The Exorcist does a great job cultivating an atmosphere of fear, but The Shining does so in a way that makes you feel like you stumbled upon a tape of true to life found footage from a haunted hotel years ago. Which film techniques give The Shining this quality, and why is that so hard to find in other films?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The angles and the camera movements are off just slightly. There are continuity errors. There are some really symmetrical shots which in the context of a horror, is quite unique. The camera just drifts like a ghost too. It’s also edited with some very jarring jump cuts. It’s a masterpiece of atmosphere
  - Absolutely amazing set design, possibly the greatest ever for a film that is mostly mundane. You take that and film it in ways to make rooms seem larger, empty and combine it with all the low angle shots from Danny's point of view and it's just *chef's kiss*.


It's an infinitely rewatchable film from one of the masters of the art form. Can't count how many time I've seen it and there's always something new to pick up on.
  - > The camera just drifts like a ghost too.

The free-floating movement and lingering shots were actually inspired by Kubrick’s love of Eraserhead, if I remember correctly. Definitely gives it that dreamy quality.
    - I thought maybe you'd made that up, but sure enough, Kubrick was crazy about Eraserhead. I mean, it does make sense. 

[https://youtu.be/VJ4Fa-HRb3I?si=-l46ClWVDlDBpOpa](https://youtu.be/VJ4Fa-HRb3I?si=-l46ClWVDlDBpOpa)

So basically, Eraserhead was loved by both Kubrick \*and\* Mel Brooks!
  - So much of the first half of the movie is just formalities, the characters having polite conversations with each other— the interview, taking the tour of the hotel. But the camerawork instills so much dread, even though you know what’s going to happen. Everything just feels a little off, like you said.

I honestly find the first half of the film creepier for this reason.
2. Kubrick purposely had props move around for purposeful continuity errors, had an impossible layout for the hotel, and had camera angles that just felt off, as well as angles that looked perfect and out of place in a standard horror, along with the haunting score. He did a great job of making you subconsciously feel uneasy.
  - Also the famous carpet was designed to be uneasy on the eyes and cause distress.
3. I think the pioneering use of the Steadicam was one of the big reasons for what you’re describing. The floating feeling, it’s almost magical or supernatural. 

Also, the false geography of the Overlook—as discussed in *Room 237*—wherein there are spaces seen in the film that should not exist according to what we see in other scenes. (An example is the odd window behind the guy interviewing Jack; the office is in the middle of the building where there could be no windows.) I think this also makes the place feel haunted.
4. I know what you mean, it feels like Kubrick was channeling spirits through the film. There is a heavy, brooding quality to it, right from the first shot. I think the music and sound design greatly contribute. If you notice, the music and ghostly chants ramp up over the course of the film, particularly during the climax, when everything is falling a part. There's also that metallic tinnitus sound to signify "the shining."

The hotel feels like a character as well  because the hotels design is so singular and iconic, and also because the people you meet, like Lloyd and Grady, feel more like aspects of the hotel than individual characters. 

It's funny you mention The Exorcist, because I associate that one, The Shining and Rosemary's Baby. They don't feel like a campy thrill, or something to shock you just to get a reaction. It feels like there's genuinely evil energy to them.
5. To me, the genius of the movie is that Kubrick extends the uneasiness of the movie to the audience in a unique way: you feel there's something wrong with the movie itself. It's like when you're driving somewhere and your car makes a funny noise. You think, "Wait, that's not supposed to happen..."

All through the movie, there are things that are wrong. Scenes are shot from awkward angles, the editing seems haphazard. There are many, many continuity errors. The entire layout of the hotel makes no sense. So many things you see on a second watch, but on a first watch you still feel that sense of wrongness even if you didn't notice everything.

The movie makes you uneasy because there's something wrong with the movie itself. It doesn't behave the way a movie should. You find yourself thinking about watching the movie while you're watching the movie. The room you're sitting in becomes part of the movie experience. With Kubrick's expert handling, you become part of the Overlook Hotel, watching these events play out from schizophrenic angles.

## 2. "The Road Warrior" & "Fury Road" are All About Processing Emotional Trauma

**Score:** 6
**Body:** The Mad Max films are some of my all-time favorites. My personal favorite entry being "Fury Road," but "The Road Warrior" is a close second. What draws me into these films so much is the character of Max. Whether portrayed by Mel Gibson or Tom Hardy, Max is a man haunted by his past, constantly running from emotional wounds, who eventually learns to process that pain in order to launch himself into a greater masculinity.

Max is a classic "anti-hero." Apathetic. Selfish. Lonely. And very capable. He doesn't need other people to get around, and, in fact, he's quite \*afraid\* of others. It's odd, right? For a man who is shown off to be so stoic and macho and tough (see the beginning of "The Road Warrior"), he sure does shrink and cower when met with... the prospect of intimate human connection. As soon as the people inhabiting the fuel depot show some fondness for Max, he draws away instinctively, letting them know right away that he plans on leaving. The leader's pleas and speeches cannot pierce Max's cold heart; he is Hell-bent on getting away from these civilized people ASAP.

There's hardly anything that Max is shown to be truly \*scared\* of in "The Road Warrior." Not raiders, not the wasteland. But people--civilized, decent people--do scare him. Isn't that odd? Why does such a strong man have this phobia of fellowship? He rushes off as soon as a group of okay people communicate a fondness for him, and even offer him a place in their family. Why?

Max is a man haunted by his past. The pain of losing his wife and daughter is too much for him to handle. He hasn't processed the grief properly, and so these unresolved emotions lead him to avoid human connection entirely. The reason he is afraid to accept their invitation initially is because intimacy with others is only a reminder of what he once had--and tragically lost. He associates love and connection with his wife and daughter, and as they are gone and he hasn't mourned them properly, he runs from others. Nobody can remind him of what he has lost if he keeps to himself forever.

But as we see in "Fury Road," Max's tendency to self-isolate leads to a mental downward-spiral, to a point of illness. The film opens with Max hearing the voices of his daughter in his head. The delusions eat away at him, mock him, torture him.

Max has to grieve. He has to confront what happened before, the tragedies he's endured, and only though this process of grieving can he overcome the onset of insanity, and move forward as a man.

"If you can't fix what's broken... you'll go insane."

Please consider giving my video a watch (link here: https://youtu.be/w4g06D2bA0c). But anyways, thanks for reading, and have a great day!

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I recently purchased The Road Warrior on 4K, not having watched it for decades (and on an analog TV screen).  The picture was terrific, but I was "shocked" at how much I enjoyed the movie, which I had seen before.  It was a very cleanly constructed, action adventure film.  The director wasn't trying to make some preachy point and make the film contort to "the message".  The viewer can "lose" oneself just enjoying the movie.  

It is certainly worth checking out, even if you've previously dismissed the notion of buying the 4K because it may not have left a strong impression in previous viewings.

---
